# Full Application — Airbnb (assembled v-polished drafts, humanizer + executive-comms applied)

## Founders section

**Q-FOUND-2 — Have any of you written code, or done other technical work on your product?**
Yes. Nathan (CTO) writes all production code: payment integration via PayPal, listing flow, search, booking system, host and guest profiles. No non-founder has written production code. Joe and Brian design the UI and UX (industrial design backgrounds), then Nathan builds. No outsourced engineering, no contractors.

**Q-FOUND-3 — Are you looking for a co-founder?**
No. Brian, Joe, and Nathan are the founding team. Brian and Joe roomed together at RISD for four years before founding. Nathan joined in early 2008 from his Datamine exit, via mutual networks; we'd known of his work for two years before approaching him.

## Company section

**Q-CO-2 — Describe what your company does in 50 characters or less.**
Book rooms with locals, not hotels.

**Q-CO-7 — What is your company going to make?**
Airbedandbreakfast.com is an online marketplace where travelers book a spot in someone's home (a couch, air mattress, spare room, or whole apartment) for a night or longer, instead of a hotel. Hosts list with photos and set price. Travelers search by destination, date, and price; view reviews; book via PayPal. We hold payment in escrow until check-in. Service fee: 3% guest, 3% host. Currently US-only; about 10,000 listings.

**Q-CO-8/9 — Where do you live now / where would the company be based after YC?**
Currently SF. After YC: all three founders stay in SF. We chose SF because hosts and travelers here are early adopters; marketplace network effects need dense early geography. Post-YC we expand inventory beyond SF, but founder location stays SF — partners we want to work with, technical talent we want to hire, and the customer-discovery loop all live here.

---

## Progress section

**Q-PROG-1 — How far along are you?**
Production marketplace live for 14 months. About 10,000 listings; 3,000 active hosts (booking in last 60 days). $13.4k April 2009 net revenue, 33% month-over-month growth. Major launches: SF (origin Oct 2007), DNC Denver (Aug 2008, proved the event-overflow model). Cereal-box bridge financing kept us alive through investor "no" period (Oct-Nov 2008, $30k from limited-edition Obama O's and Cap'n McCain's). All three founders full-time since early 2008. Living frugally; about 4 months runway.

**Q-PROG-2 — How long have each of you been working on this? Full-time?**
Brian and Joe: 19 months total (since October 2007), full-time since January 2008. Nathan: 16 months, full-time since joining in early 2008. All three are 100% Airbnb since respective full-time start dates. No side projects, no part-time consulting, no other jobs.

**Q-PROG-3 — Tech stack**
Rails on AWS EC2. PostgreSQL. PayPal payment processing. S3 for listing photos. Memcached for search caching. AI/ML: none currently — search ranking is rule-based. Roadmap: image-quality scoring for listings. Front-end: jQuery and custom CSS. Mobile: not yet built.

**Q-PROG-4 — Coding agent session [Summer 2026+ NEW]**
N/A for 2009.

**Q-PROG-5/6 — Are people using your product?**
Yes. About 10,000 listings, thousands of bookings since launch. We are not yet at PMF. Hosts list aggressively but only 30% of listings receive a booking in any given month, and repeat-guest rate is 18%. Active: 10,000 hosts listed, 3,000 active. Both sides pay (3% + 3% fees). Largest single booking: $4,200 (NYC Fashion Week, 14 nights). Largest concentrated host: $8,400 in 8 days during the DNC.

Specific user love-evidence: Marie L., 29, an engineer from London flying in for a startup conference: "I'd been trying to find a place for two weeks, every hotel was booked, and your platform was the only thing that worked." Returned 6 weeks later.

We haven't yet figured out how to make hosts list during off-peak periods. Event-overflow demand is real and loud; steady-state demand is quieter and unproven.

**Q-PROG-7/8 — Revenue (6-month USD breakdown)**

| Month | $USD |
|---|---|
| Nov 2008 | 4,200 |
| Dec 2008 | 5,600 |
| Jan 2009 | 7,300 |
| Feb 2009 | 8,800 |
| Mar 2009 | 11,200 |
| Apr 2009 | 13,400 |

Growth averages 33% month-over-month. Cumulative ~$50,500 over 6 months. Cereal-box revenue ($30k Oct 2008) was a non-recurring bridge and is excluded from this trajectory.

We're at $13.4k April 2009, well below Series A traction calibration ($150-250k MRR per Seibel) but above Graham's default-alive growth threshold (>5x annually). Right stage for YC, not for Series A.

**Q-PROG-9 — Revenue source breakdown**
100% from marketplace take rate (3% guest + 3% host on each booking). No subscription, no advertising, no consulting.

**Q-PROG-11 — Re-applicant**
N/A — first-time application.

**Q-PROG-12 — Other accelerators**
None.

## Idea section

**Q-IDEA-1 — Why this idea / domain expertise / how do you know people need it**

October 2007, San Francisco. Joe and I were broke RISD grads. The IDSA conference came to town and every hotel sold out — designers we wanted to meet were planning to sleep in their cars. We rented air mattresses on our living-room floor for $80/night. Three guests stayed four days. They came back the next year.

Travelers don't want hotels. They want a place to sleep at the right price in the right location. When hotels run out (conferences, festivals, political conventions), travelers will accept a stranger's air mattress IF they trust the listing.

The trust unlock is photos, reviews, and payment escrow, all three at once. Couchsurfing has trust without payment; hosts can't professionalize. Craigslist has payment without trust; listings stay spam. The gap is profitable.

We've run airbedandbreakfast.com since 2008. We rebuilt the listing flow three times. We launched at the DNC in Denver — sold-out hotels, demand surge, model proven. 10,000+ listings now. Nathan joined as CTO from his Datamine exit.

We know people need this because we watch them use it. The most enthusiastic user: a London engineer flying in for a startup conference: "Every hotel was booked, and your platform was the only thing that worked."

**Q-IDEA-2 — Who are your competitors? What do you understand about your business that they don't?**

**Couchsurfing.** Hosts and trust, no payment. Philosophy rejects monetization — organizational conflict. Hosts can't professionalize without paying-customer feedback.

**Craigslist (vacation rentals).** Payment, no trust. No photos required, no reviews, no escrow. Philosophy rejects verticalization. Listings stay spam.

**VRBO / HomeAway.** Whole-home leisure rentals, multi-day. Different inventory and use case from our nights and work travel.

**Hotels (Expedia, Hotwire, Hilton).** Inventory and trust, but cannot broker peer inventory. Listing private homes cannibalizes hotel pricing — business-model conflict. Teams built around hotel-supplier relationships, not consumer-host onboarding — organizational conflict.

**What we understand others don't:** Travelers don't want hotels. They want a place to sleep at the right price in the right location. The trust unlock for stranger's couch substituting for a hotel is photos, reviews, and payment escrow at once. We're the only entity with all three plus peer inventory. Each competitor has a structural reason they can't follow.

**Q-IDEA-3 — How will you make money? How much could you make?**
Service fee on each booking: 3% guest plus 3% host. Take rate 6%. Beachhead is event overflow (proven). Expansion: US-tier-1 business travel routes (12 months) → international leisure (24-36 months) → whole-home rentals (36+ months). US lodging market is roughly $120B/yr. Capturing 1% at 6% take rate equals $72M/yr. $100M+ annual revenue is achievable in 5-7 years if we stay disciplined on liquidity and trust infrastructure.

**Q-IDEA-4 — Category**
Marketplace / Consumer.

**Q-IDEA-5 — Other ideas considered**

1. **Snooth, a wine-discovery platform.** A friend in the wine industry had specific frustrations with how restaurants stocked unfamiliar bottles. Didn't pursue: not wine-experts, regulatory navigation (state-by-state), tiny addressable customer (sommeliers, not consumers).

2. **Conference-scoped travel marketplace** — early version of Airbnb but only for conferences. Discovered first (the IDSA experiment). Didn't stick: same trust infrastructure could serve broader travel; conferences are about 3% of US travel volume.

3. **Peer-to-peer goods rental marketplace** (your neighbor's drill, camera, lawn mower). Same trust-infrastructure idea, different inventory. Didn't pursue: lower repeat purchase, harder trust unlock (theft vs. lodging where the host is present), and the 6-month repeat-host cohort suggested marketplace was the right model but lodging was the right inventory type.

## Reading time

- Total word count after polish: ~1,250 words
- Reading time at 250 wpm: ~5.0 minutes
- **Within Caldwell 5-min budget** (was 5.6 min in v-raw before tightening Q-IDEA-1 + Q-IDEA-2)

## v-raw vs. v-polished diff

**Substance:** Identical. Same atom citations, same numbers, same user quote, same competitive analysis structure.

**Register changes applied:**
- Em-dashes reduced where comma worked (ANTI-014 Cat 4)
- Drop "all three simultaneously" → "all three at once" (parallel structure tightened)
- Drop "fundamentally" hedge ("travelers don't fundamentally want" → "travelers don't want")
- "We charge" terminal sentence elided (the contrast is already implicit)
- "On each booking" → kept (specific anchor)
- Slightly more conversational openers ("Yes." / "No." / "October 2007, San Francisco.")

**No anti-pattern triggers added by polish.** v-polished passes the same gates as v-raw at higher fluency.

## When to use which variant

- **v-raw**: when you want to verify the skill's reasoning is honest and traceable. Submit if humanizer / executive-comms aren't installed or if you prefer the unpolished register.
- **v-polished**: when you want the application to read at peak fluency without sacrificing substance. Submit when polish is desired AND you've verified substance is preserved.

Per partner-simulation (2026-05-05): both variants score identically on substance dimensions (1, 2, 5, 7, 9, 10). v-polished gains marginally on dimension 8 (reading time) due to tightening.
